questions.questionText:
Let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}}=10 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}$ and a vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$ be such that $2(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}})+3(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}})=\overrightarrow{0}$.

If $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=15$, then $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \cdot(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{k})$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
The unknown vector $\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}}$ is represented in 3D: $\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}}=\boldsymbol{x} \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+\boldsymbol{y} \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+z \hat{\boldsymbol{k}}$

Cross product of 2 vectors $\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{a}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{b}}$ :

$$ (\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{b}})=\left|\begin{array}{l|r|r} \hat{\boldsymbol{i}} & \hat{\boldsymbol{j}} & \hat{\boldsymbol{k}} \tag{i}\\ 4 & -1 & 3 \\ 10 & 2 & -1 \end{array}\right|=-5 \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+34 \hat{j}+18 \hat{\boldsymbol{k}} \ldots . . (i)$$

Cross product of 2 vectors $\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{b}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}}$ :

$$ (\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{b}} \times \overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}})=\left|\begin{array}{l|l|r} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 10 & 2 & -1 \\ \boldsymbol{x} & \boldsymbol{y} & \boldsymbol{z} \end{array}\right|=(2 z+y) \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-(10 z+\boldsymbol{x}) \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+(10 \boldsymbol{y}-2 \boldsymbol{x}) \hat{\boldsymbol{k}} \ldots \ldots(\boldsymbol{i} \boldsymbol{i}) $$

We know, $2(\boldsymbol{a} \times \boldsymbol{b})+3(\boldsymbol{b} \times \boldsymbol{c})=0$

$\Rightarrow 2(-5 \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+34 \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+18 \hat{\boldsymbol{k}})+3\{(2 \boldsymbol{z}+\boldsymbol{y}) \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-(10 \boldsymbol{z}+x) \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+(10 \boldsymbol{y}-2 x) \hat{\boldsymbol{k}}\}=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $(6 z+3 y-10) \hat{i}+(68-30 z-3 x) j+(36+30 y-6 x) \hat{k}=0$

As individual vector components of a null vector are zero.

$\begin{aligned} \boldsymbol{x}-5 \boldsymbol{y} & =6 \ldots . \boldsymbol{P}_1 \\ \boldsymbol{y}+2 \boldsymbol{z} & =\frac{10}{3} \ldots . . \boldsymbol{P}_2 \\ 10 \boldsymbol{z}+\boldsymbol{x} & =\frac{68}{3} \ldots . \boldsymbol{P}_3\end{aligned}$

$(\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{c})=15 \Rightarrow(4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \cdot(x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k})=4 x-y+3 z=15 \ldots \ldots(i v)$

Equation set $(i i i)$ is mathematically constructed to form a linear combination of 2 planes $\boldsymbol{P}_1$ and $\boldsymbol{P}_2$ generating the $3^{\text {rd }}$ plane $\boldsymbol{P}_3$ :

$$ \boldsymbol{x}-5 \boldsymbol{y}+(\boldsymbol{\lambda}) \cdot(\boldsymbol{y}+2 \boldsymbol{z})=\frac{68}{3} \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{\lambda}=5 \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{P}_1+5 \boldsymbol{P}_2=\boldsymbol{P}_3 \ldots \ldots(\boldsymbol{v}) $$

Equation ( $\boldsymbol{v}$ ) is conclusive in generating a family of planes $\left\{\boldsymbol{P}_1, \boldsymbol{P}_2, \boldsymbol{P}_3\right\}$ indicating infinite solutions for the system equation (iii)

We need an auxiliary equation provided through (iv) to render an unique solution for the vector $\vec{c}$ :

$\left[\begin{array}{rlrr}x-5 y & =6 & \ldots . & P_1 \\ y+2 z & =\frac{10}{3} & \ldots . & P_2 \\ 4 x-y+3 z & =15 & \ldots . & P_4\end{array}\right] \ldots .(v i)$

Equation (vi) can be solved using Determinants and Cramer's rule :

$$ \boldsymbol{\Delta}=\left|\begin{array}{r|r|r} 1 & -5 & 0 \tag{vii}\\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 4 & -1 & 3 \end{array}\right| \quad \leftarrow \quad \boldsymbol{R}_3=\boldsymbol{R}_3-4 \boldsymbol{R}_1=\left|\begin{array}{r|r|r} 1 & -5 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 19 & 3 \end{array}\right|=-35 \quad \ldots . . (vii)$$

$\Delta_x=\left|\begin{array}{|l|r|l|}6 & -5 & 0 \\ \frac{10}{3} & 1 & 2 \\ 15 & -1 & 3\end{array}\right|$

$\begin{aligned} & \boldsymbol{R}_1=\boldsymbol{R}_1+5 \boldsymbol{R}_2 \\ & \boldsymbol{R}_3=\boldsymbol{R}_3+\boldsymbol{R}_2\end{aligned}$

$=\left|\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\frac{68}{3} & 0 & 10 \\ \frac{10}{3} & 1 & 2 \\ \frac{55}{3} & 0 & 5\end{array}\right|$

$C_1 \leftrightarrows C_2$

$=-\left|\begin{array}{l|l}0 & \frac{68}{3} \\ 1 & \frac{10}{3} \\ 0 & \frac{10}{3} \\ \frac{55}{3}\end{array}\right|=\left(\frac{68}{3}\right) \cdot(5)-\left(\frac{55}{3}\right) \cdot(10)=-70 \ldots \ldots(v i i i)$

Equations $(\boldsymbol{v i i}),(\boldsymbol{v i i i}),(\boldsymbol{i x})$ and $(\boldsymbol{x})$ provides the solution to system equation $(\boldsymbol{v i})$ from Cramer's rule :

$\left[\begin{array}{l}x=\frac{\Delta_x}{\Delta}=\frac{70}{35}= \\ y=\frac{\Delta_y}{\Delta}=-\frac{28}{35}=-\frac{4}{5} \\ z=\frac{\Delta_z}{\Delta}=\frac{217}{(3) \cdot(35)}=\frac{31}{15}\end{array}\right]$

$\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}}=(2) \hat{\boldsymbol{i}}-\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}+\left(\frac{31}{15}\right) \hat{\boldsymbol{k}}$

$\begin{aligned} & \therefore(\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{c}}) \cdot(\hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{\boldsymbol{k}}) \\ & =\left\{(2) \hat{i}-\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) \hat{j}+\left(\frac{31}{15}\right) \hat{k}\right\} \cdot(\hat{\boldsymbol{i}}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{\boldsymbol{k}}) \\ & =2-\frac{4}{5}-\frac{31}{5}\end{aligned}$

$=-\frac{4+31-10}{5} =-5$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: Let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$. Then the square of the area of the triangle with adjacent sides determined by the vectors $(2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b})$ and $(\vec{a}-\vec{b})$ is :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Given

$$ \vec{a} = 2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 3\hat{k}, \qquad \vec{b} = 6\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 3\hat{k} $$

We need the square of the area of the triangle whose adjacent sides are

$$ 2\vec{a} + 3\vec{b} \quad \text{and} \quad \vec{a} - \vec{b} $$

For two adjacent sides $\vec{u}$ and $\vec{v}$, area of triangle is

$$ \frac{1}{2} |\vec{u} \times \vec{v}| $$

So, square of the area is

$$ \left( \frac{1}{2} |\vec{u} \times \vec{v}| \right)^2 = \frac{1}{4} |\vec{u} \times \vec{v}|^2 $$

Let

$$ \vec{u} = 2\vec{a} + 3\vec{b}, \qquad \vec{v} = \vec{a} - \vec{b} $$

First find $\vec{u}$:

$$ 2\vec{a} = 2(2\hat{i}+3\hat{j}+3\hat{k}) = 4\hat{i}+6\hat{j}+6\hat{k} $$

$$ 3\vec{b} = 3(6\hat{i}+3\hat{j}+3\hat{k}) = 18\hat{i}+9\hat{j}+9\hat{k} $$

Therefore,

$$ \vec{u} = 2\vec{a}+3\vec{b} = 22\hat{i}+15\hat{j}+15\hat{k} $$

Now find $\vec{v}$:

$$ \vec{v} = \vec{a}-\vec{b} = (2-6)\hat{i} + (3-3)\hat{j} + (3-3)\hat{k} = -4\hat{i} $$

Now compute $\vec{u} \times \vec{v}$:

$$ \vec{u} \times \vec{v} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 22 & 15 & 15 \\ -4 & 0 & 0 \end{vmatrix} $$

Expanding,

$$ \vec{u} \times \vec{v} = \hat{i}(15\cdot 0 - 15\cdot 0) - \hat{j}(22\cdot 0 - 15\cdot (-4)) + \hat{k}(22\cdot 0 - 15\cdot (-4)) $$

$$ = 0\hat{i} - 60\hat{j} + 60\hat{k} $$

So,

$$ |\vec{u} \times \vec{v}|^2 = 0^2 + (-60)^2 + 60^2 = 3600 + 3600 = 7200 $$

Hence square of the area of the triangle is

$$ \frac{1}{4} \times 7200 = 1800 $$

Therefore, the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{1800} $$

So, Option C is correct.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: If $\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{b}=\hat{j}-\hat{k}$ and $\vec{c}$ be three vectors such that $\vec{a} \times \vec{c}=\vec{b}$ and $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=3$, then $\vec{c} \cdot(\vec{a}-2 \vec{b})$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Let

$$ \vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \qquad \vec{b}=\hat{j}-\hat{k} $$

We have to find

$$ \vec{c}\cdot (\vec{a}-2\vec{b}) $$

given that

$$ \vec{a}\times \vec{c}=\vec{b} \quad \text{and} \quad \vec{a}\cdot \vec{c}=3 $$

Let

$$ \vec{c}=x\hat{i}+y\hat{j}+z\hat{k} $$

Then

$$ \vec{a}=(1,1,1), \qquad \vec{b}=(0,1,-1) $$

Now compute $\vec{a}\times \vec{c}$:

$$ \vec{a}\times \vec{c} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k}\\ 1 & 1 & 1\\ x & y & z \end{vmatrix} $$

Expanding,

$$ \vec{a}\times \vec{c} = \hat{i}(z-y)-\hat{j}(z-x)+\hat{k}(y-x) $$

Since this is equal to $\vec{b}=\hat{j}-\hat{k}$, we compare components:

$$ z-y=0 $$

$$ -(z-x)=1 \Rightarrow x-z=1 $$

$$ y-x=-1 $$

Also, from $\vec{a}\cdot \vec{c}=3$,

$$ x+y+z=3 $$

Now solve the equations.

From $z-y=0$,

$$ z=y $$

From $x-z=1$,

$$ x=y+1 $$

Substitute into $x+y+z=3$:

$$ (y+1)+y+y=3 $$

$$ 3y+1=3 $$

$$ 3y=2 \Rightarrow y=\frac{2}{3} $$

So,

$$ z=\frac{2}{3}, \qquad x=\frac{5}{3} $$

Hence,

$$ \vec{c}=\frac{5}{3}\hat{i}+\frac{2}{3}\hat{j}+\frac{2}{3}\hat{k} $$

Now find $\vec{a}-2\vec{b}$:

$$ \vec{a}-2\vec{b} = (\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})-2(\hat{j}-\hat{k}) $$

$$ = \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3\hat{k} $$

Therefore,

$$ \vec{c}\cdot(\vec{a}-2\vec{b}) = \left(\frac{5}{3},\frac{2}{3},\frac{2}{3}\right)\cdot(1,-1,3) $$

$$ = \frac{5}{3}-\frac{2}{3}+2 $$

$$ = \frac{3}{3}+2=1+2=3 $$

So, the required value is

$$ \boxed{3} $$

questions.questionText: Let $O$ be the origin, $\overrightarrow{O P}=\vec{a}$ and $\overrightarrow{O Q}=\vec{b}$. If $R$ is the point on $\overrightarrow{O P}$ such that $\overrightarrow{O P}=5 \overrightarrow{O R}$, and $M$ is the point such that $\overrightarrow{O Q}=5 \overrightarrow{R M}$, then $\overrightarrow{P M}$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: Let $\vec{a}=\sqrt{7} \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}$ and $\vec{b}=\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$. If $\vec{r}$ is a vector such that $\vec{r} \times \vec{a}+\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\overrightarrow{0}$ and $\vec{r} \cdot \vec{a}=0$, then $|3 \vec{r}|^2$ is equal to:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Given

$$ \vec{a}=\sqrt{7}\,\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}, \qquad \vec{b}=\hat{j}+2\hat{k} $$

and

$$ \vec{r}\times \vec{a}+\vec{a}\times \vec{b}=\vec{0}, \qquad \vec{r}\cdot \vec{a}=0 $$

We have to find $|3\vec{r}|^2$.

From

$$ \vec{r}\times \vec{a}+\vec{a}\times \vec{b}=\vec{0} $$

note that

$$ \vec{a}\times \vec{b}=-(\vec{b}\times \vec{a}) $$

So,

$$ \vec{r}\times \vec{a}=\vec{b}\times \vec{a} $$

Hence,

$$ (\vec{r}-\vec{b})\times \vec{a}=0 $$

This means $\vec{r}-\vec{b}$ is parallel to $\vec{a}$. Therefore,

$$ \vec{r}=\vec{b}+\lambda \vec{a} $$

for some scalar $\lambda$.

Now use the condition

$$ \vec{r}\cdot \vec{a}=0 $$

Substitute $\vec{r}=\vec{b}+\lambda \vec{a}$:

$$ (\vec{b}+\lambda \vec{a})\cdot \vec{a}=0 $$

So,

$$ \vec{b}\cdot \vec{a}+\lambda (\vec{a}\cdot \vec{a})=0 $$

Now calculate these dot products.

1. Find $\vec{b}\cdot \vec{a}$

$$ \vec{b}=(0,1,2), \qquad \vec{a}=(\sqrt{7},1,-1) $$

Thus,

$$ \vec{b}\cdot \vec{a}=0\cdot \sqrt{7}+1\cdot 1+2\cdot(-1)=1-2=-1 $$

2. Find $\vec{a}\cdot \vec{a}$

$$ \vec{a}\cdot \vec{a}=(\sqrt{7})^2+1^2+(-1)^2=7+1+1=9 $$

Therefore,

$$ -1+9\lambda=0 $$

So,

$$ \lambda=\frac{1}{9} $$

Hence,

$$ \vec{r}=\vec{b}+\frac{1}{9}\vec{a} $$

Now find $|\vec{r}|^2$.

Using

$$ \vec{r}=\vec{b}+\frac{1}{9}\vec{a} $$

we get

$$ |\vec{r}|^2=\left|\vec{b}+\frac{1}{9}\vec{a}\right|^2 $$

Expand:

$$ |\vec{r}|^2=|\vec{b}|^2+\frac{1}{81}|\vec{a}|^2+\frac{2}{9}(\vec{a}\cdot \vec{b}) $$

Now,

$$ |\vec{b}|^2=0^2+1^2+2^2=5 $$

$$ |\vec{a}|^2=9 $$

$$ \vec{a}\cdot \vec{b}=-1 $$

So,

$$ |\vec{r}|^2=5+\frac{9}{81}+\frac{2}{9}(-1) $$

$$ =5+\frac{1}{9}-\frac{2}{9} =5-\frac{1}{9} =\frac{44}{9} $$

Therefore,

$$ |3\vec{r}|^2=9|\vec{r}|^2=9\cdot \frac{44}{9}=44 $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{44} $$

Hence, Option A.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: Let $\hat{u}$ and $\hat{v}$ be unit vectors inclined at an acute angle such that $|\hat{u} \times \hat{v}|=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. If $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}=\lambda \hat{u}+\hat{v}+(\hat{u} \times \hat{v})$, then $\lambda$ is equal to:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Since $\hat{u}$ and $\hat{v}$ are unit vectors,

$$ |\hat{u}\times \hat{v}|=|\hat{u}||\hat{v}|\sin\theta=\sin\theta $$

Given

$$ |\hat{u}\times \hat{v}|=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} $$

So,

$$ \sin\theta=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} $$

The angle is acute, hence

$$ \theta=60^\circ \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{u}\cdot \hat{v}=\cos 60^\circ=\frac{1}{2} $$

Now,

$$ \vec A=\lambda \hat{u}+\hat{v}+(\hat{u}\times \hat{v}) $$

We take dot product with $\hat{u}$:

$$ \vec A\cdot \hat{u} = \lambda (\hat{u}\cdot \hat{u})+(\hat{v}\cdot \hat{u})+(\hat{u}\times \hat{v})\cdot \hat{u} $$

Now,

$$ \hat{u}\cdot \hat{u}=1,\qquad \hat{v}\cdot \hat{u}=\frac{1}{2},\qquad (\hat{u}\times \hat{v})\cdot \hat{u}=0 $$

Therefore,

$$ \vec A\cdot \hat{u}=\lambda+\frac{1}{2} $$

So,

$$ \lambda=\vec A\cdot \hat{u}-\frac{1}{2} \qquad \text{(1)} $$

Now take dot product with $\hat{v}$:

$$ \vec A\cdot \hat{v} = \lambda (\hat{u}\cdot \hat{v})+(\hat{v}\cdot \hat{v})+(\hat{u}\times \hat{v})\cdot \hat{v} $$

Using

$$ \hat{u}\cdot \hat{v}=\frac{1}{2},\qquad \hat{v}\cdot \hat{v}=1,\qquad (\hat{u}\times \hat{v})\cdot \hat{v}=0 $$

we get

$$ \vec A\cdot \hat{v}=\frac{\lambda}{2}+1 $$

Hence,

$$ \frac{\lambda}{2}=\vec A\cdot \hat{v}-1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda=2(\vec A\cdot \hat{v})-2 \qquad \text{(2)} $$

Now let

$$ x=\vec A\cdot \hat{u}, \qquad y=\vec A\cdot \hat{v} $$

Then from above,

$$ x=\lambda+\frac{1}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad y=\frac{\lambda}{2}+1 $$

From the first,

$$ \lambda=x-\frac{1}{2} $$

Also,

$$ x-2y=\left(\lambda+\frac{1}{2}\right)-2\left(\frac{\lambda}{2}+1\right) =\lambda+\frac{1}{2}-\lambda-2=-\frac{3}{2} $$

So we form $\lambda$ as a linear combination of $x$ and $y$.

Check Option A:

$$ \frac{4}{3}x-\frac{2}{3}y = \frac{4}{3}\left(\lambda+\frac{1}{2}\right)-\frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{\lambda}{2}+1\right) $$

$$ = \frac{4\lambda}{3}+\frac{2}{3}-\frac{\lambda}{3}-\frac{2}{3} = \lambda $$

Hence,

$$ \lambda=\frac{4}{3}(\vec A\cdot \hat{u})-\frac{2}{3}(\vec A\cdot \hat{v}) $$

So the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option A}} $$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText:
Let the vectors $\vec{a} = -\hat{i} + \hat{j} + 3\hat{k}$ and $\vec{b} = \hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + \hat{k}$. For some $\lambda, \mu \in \mathbb{R}$, let $\vec{c} = \lambda \vec{a} + \mu \vec{b}$.

If $\vec{c} \cdot (3\hat{i} - 6\hat{j} + 2\hat{k}) = 10$ and $\vec{c} \cdot (\hat{i} + \hat{j} + \hat{k}) = -2$, then $|\vec{c}|^2$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: Two adjacent sides of a parallelogram PQRS are given by $\overrightarrow{PQ} = \hat{j} + \hat{k}$ and $\overrightarrow{PS} = \hat{i} - \hat{j}$. If the side PS is rotated about the point P by an acute angle $\alpha$ in the plane of the parallelogram so that it becomes perpendicular to the side PQ, then $\sin^2\left(\frac{5\alpha}{2}\right) - \sin^2\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)$ is equal to:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: If $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ are two vectors such that $|\vec{a}|=2$ and $|\vec{b}|=3$, then the maximum value of $3|(3 \vec{a}+2 \vec{b})|+4|(3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b})|$ is :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: Let P be a point in the plane of the vectors $\overrightarrow{AB}=3\hat{i} + \hat{j} - \hat{k}$ and $\overrightarrow{AC}=\hat{i} - \hat{j} + 3\hat{k}$ such that P is equidistant from the lines AB and AC. If $|\overrightarrow{AP}| = \frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}$, then the area of the triangle ABP is :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Point $P$ is equidistant from the lines $A B$ and $A C$. Geometrically, this means point $P$ must lie on the angle bisector of the angle formed by vectors $A B$ and $A C$.

First, we find the unit vectors along $A B$ and $A C$ :

  • $|A B|=\sqrt{3^2+1^2+(-1)^2}=\sqrt{11}$

  • $|A C|=\sqrt{1^2+(-1)^2+3^2}=\sqrt{11}$

Since the magnitudes are equal, the angle bisector $v$ is simply the sum of the two vectors:

$$ \begin{array}{r} v=A B+A C=(3+1) \hat{i}+(1-1) \hat{j}+(-1+3) \hat{k}=4 \hat{i}+2 \hat{k} \end{array} $$

The vector $A P$ will be in the direction of the unit vector $\hat{v}$ :

$$ \hat{v}=\frac{4 \hat{i}+2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{4^2+2^2}}=\frac{4 \hat{i}+2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{20}}=\frac{4 \hat{i}+2 \hat{k}}{2 \sqrt{5}}=\frac{2 \hat{i}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{5}} $$

Determine Vector $A P$

Given that $|A P|=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}$, we can write:

$$ A P=|A P| \hat{v}=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}\left(\frac{2 \hat{i}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{5}}\right)=\frac{1}{2}(2 \hat{i}+\hat{k})=\hat{i}+\frac{1}{2} \hat{k} $$

  1. Calculate the Area of Triangle $A B P$

The area of a triangle formed by two vectors $A B$ and $A P$ is given by the formula:

$$ \text { Area }=\frac{1}{2}|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A P}| $$

First, compute the cross product $A B \times A P$ :

$$ \begin{gathered} \overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A P}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 & \frac{1}{2} \end{array}\right| \\ =\hat{i}\left(\frac{1}{2}-0\right)-\hat{j}\left(\frac{3}{2}-(-1)\right)+\hat{k}(0-1) \\ =\frac{1}{2} \hat{i}-\frac{5}{2} \hat{j}-\hat{k} \\ |\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A P}|=\sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+\left(-\frac{5}{2}\right)^2+(-1)^2}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}+\frac{25}{4}+1}=\sqrt{\frac{26}{4}+\frac{4}{4}}=\sqrt{\frac{30}{4}}=\frac{\sqrt{30}}{2} \end{gathered} $$

Finally, the area of the triangle is:

Area $=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\sqrt{30}}{2}\right)=\frac{\sqrt{30}}{4} $
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: If the distance of the point $P(43, \alpha, \beta)$, $\beta < 0$, from the line $\vec{r} = 4\hat{i} - \hat{k} + \mu (2\hat{i} + 3\hat{k}), \mu \in \mathbb{R}$ along a line with direction ratios $3, -1, 0$ is $13\sqrt{10}$, then $\alpha^2 + \beta^2$ is equal to ________
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
• Point $P$ : $(43, \alpha, \beta)$ with the constraint $\beta<0$.

  • Target Line ( $L_1$ ) : Given by $\vec{r}=4 \hat{i}-\hat{k}+\mu(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k})$.

    A general point $Q$ on this line is $(4+2 \mu, 0,-1+3 \mu)$.

  • Direction of measurement : The distance is measured along a line with direction ratios $(3,-1,0)$.

Find Point $Q$ on the Target Line

Since the distance is measured along the direction $d=(3,-1,0)$, the vector $P Q$ must be parallel to $d$.

$$ \begin{gathered} P Q=Q-P=(4+2 \mu-43, \quad 0-\alpha, \quad-1+3 \mu-\beta) \\ P Q=(2 \mu-39, \quad-\alpha, \quad 3 \mu-\beta-1) \\ P Q=(2 \mu-39, \quad-\alpha, \quad 3 \mu-\beta-1) \end{gathered} $$

Because $P Q$ is parallel to $(3,-1,0)$, their components must be proportional :

  1. 1.$\frac{2 \mu-39}{3}=\frac{-\alpha}{-1} \Longrightarrow 2 \mu-39=3 \alpha \Longrightarrow 2 \mu=3 \alpha+39$
  1. 2.$\frac{3 \mu-\beta-1}{0}$ suggests the numerator must be zero for a finite ratio to exist: $3 \mu-\beta-1= 0 \Longrightarrow \beta=3 \mu-1$

Calculate the Distance $P Q$

The distance $P Q$ is given as $13 \sqrt{10}$. Using the distance formula on the proportional components where the ratio is $k$ :

$$ \begin{gathered} P Q=k(3,-1,0)=(3 k,-k, 0) \\ |P Q|=\sqrt{(3 k)^2+(-k)^2+0^2}=\sqrt{10 k^2}=|k| \sqrt{10} \end{gathered} $$

Given $|P Q|=13 \sqrt{10}$, we find $|k|=13$, so $k= \pm 13$.

Solve for $\alpha, \mu$, and $\beta$

From our proportionality equations :

  • $-\alpha=-k \Longrightarrow \alpha=k$

  • $2 \mu-39=3 k$

Case 1 : $k=13$

  • $\alpha=13$

  • $2 \mu-39=3(13)=39 \Longrightarrow 2 \mu=78 \Longrightarrow \mu=39$

  • $\beta=3 \mu-1=3(39)-1=116$

  • Constraint Check : $\beta<0$ is False. (Reject this case)

Case 2 : $k=-13$

  • $\alpha=-13$

  • $2 \mu-39=3(-13)=-39 \Longrightarrow 2 \mu=0 \Longrightarrow \mu=0$

  • $\beta=3(0)-1=-1$

  • Constraint Check : $\beta<0$ is True. (Accept this case)

$\alpha^2+\beta^2=(-13)^2+(-1)^2=169+1=170$

The value of $\alpha^2+\beta^2$ is 170 .

questions.questionText:
For three unit vectors $\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}$ satisfying

$$ |\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^2+|\vec{b}-\vec{c}|^2+|\vec{c}-\vec{a}|^2=9 \text { and }|2 \vec{a}+k \vec{b}+k \vec{c}|=3 \text {, } $$

the positive value of k is :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Given, $|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=|\vec{c}|=1$

$|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^2+|\vec{b}-\vec{c}|^2+|\vec{c}-\vec{a}|^2=9$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $|\vec{a}|^2+|\vec{b}|^2-2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+|\vec{b}|^2+|\vec{c}|^2-2 \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+|\vec{c}|^2+|\vec{a}|^2-2 \vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}=9$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $6+2(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a})=9 \quad\{|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|=|\vec{c}|=1\}$

$$ \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}=-\frac{3}{2} $$

also,

$$ |\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|^2=3+2(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}) $$

$=3+2\left(-\frac{3}{2}\right)=3-3=0$

So

$$ \vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=0 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\vec{a}+\vec{b}=-\vec{c}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $|\vec{a}+\vec{b}|^2=|\vec{c}|^2=1$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $|\vec{a}|^2+|\vec{b}|^2+2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=1$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $1+1+2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=1$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=-\frac{1}{2} $$

Similarly,

$$ \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}=-\frac{1}{2} \quad \text { and } \quad \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=-\frac{1}{2} $$

so,

$$ \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}=\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}=-\frac{1}{2} $$

It is given that

$$ |2 \vec{a}+k \vec{b}+k \vec{c}|=3 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $|2 \vec{a}+k \vec{b}+k \vec{c}|^2=9$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $4|\vec{a}|^2+k^2|\vec{b}|^2+k^2|\vec{c}|^2+4 k \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+2 k^2 \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+4 k \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=9$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $4(1)^2+k^2(1)^2+k^2(1)^2+4 k\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)+2 k^2\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)+4 k\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)=9$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $4+2 k^2-2 k-k^2-2 k=9$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $k^2-4 k-5=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $(k-5)(k+1)=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $k=5,-1$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: Let $P Q R$ be a triangle such that $\overrightarrow{P Q}=-2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PR}}=a \hat{\mathrm{i}}+b \hat{\mathrm{j}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, a, b \in \mathrm{Z}$. Let S be the point on QR , which is equidistant from the lines PQ and PR . If $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PR}}|=9$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PS}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$, then the value of $3 a-4 b$ is $\_\_\_\_$
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
We are given that $|\overrightarrow{P R}|=9$ Using the distance formula for the vector $P R=a \hat{i}+b \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}$ :

$$ \sqrt{a^2+b^2+(-4)^2}=9 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $a^2+b^2+16=81$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ a^2+b^2=65 $$

The problem states that point $S$ lies on $Q R$ and is equidistant from lines $P Q$ and $P R$. In a triangle, the locus of points equidistant from two sides is the angle bisector. Therefore, $\overrightarrow{P S}$ is the internal angle bisector of $\angle Q P R$.

For an angle bisector $\overrightarrow{P S}$, it must be collinear with the sum of the unit vectors along the adjacent sides :

$$ \overrightarrow{P S}=\lambda\left(\frac{\overrightarrow{P Q}}{|\overrightarrow{P Q}|}+\frac{\overrightarrow{P R}}{|\overrightarrow{P R}|}\right) $$

where $\lambda$ is a scalar constant.

First, calculate $|\overrightarrow{P Q}|$ :

$$ |\overrightarrow{P Q}|=\sqrt{(-2)^2+(-1)^2+2^2}=\sqrt{4+1+4}=3 $$

Now, set up the bisector equation :

$$ \overrightarrow{P S}=\lambda\left(\frac{-2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}}{3}+\frac{a \hat{i}+b \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}}{9}\right) $$

Multiply the first term by $\frac{3}{3}$ to get a common denominator:

$$ \overrightarrow{P S}=\frac{\lambda}{9}((-6+a) \hat{i}+(-3+b) \hat{j}+(6-4) \hat{k}) $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \overrightarrow{P S}=\frac{\lambda}{9}((a-6) \hat{i}+(b-3) \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) $$

We are given

$$ \overrightarrow{P S}=\hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} $$

Comparing the $\hat{k}$-components:

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \frac{\lambda}{9}(2)=2 \Rightarrow \lambda=9 $$

Substitute $\lambda=9$ into other components:

For $\hat{i}$ :

$$ a-6=1 \Rightarrow a=7 $$

For $\hat{j}$ :

$$ b-3=-7 \Rightarrow b=-4 $$

Check against the constraint :

$$ a^2+b^2=7^2+(-4)^2=49+16=65 $$

The values are consistent. value of $3 a-4 b$ :

$$ 3 a-4 b=3(7)-4(-4) $$ $=21+16=\mathbf{3 7}$

questions.questionText: Let $\vec{a}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{b}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\vec{c}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$. Let $\vec{v}$ be the vector in the plane of the vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$, such that the length of its projection on the vector $\vec{c}$ is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}$. Then $|\vec{v}|$ is equal to
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Given

$$ a=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}, b=\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}, \text { and } c=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k} . $$

$v$ is in the plane of vectors $a$ and $b$,

$$ \text { so } v=a+\lambda b=(2+\lambda) \hat{i}+(-1+3 \lambda) \hat{j}+(-1-\lambda) \hat{k} \text {. } $$

It is given that length of projection of $v$ on $c$ is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left|\frac{v \cdot c}{|c|}\right|=\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}} \\ & |v \cdot c|=\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}} \times|c| \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \quad=\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}} \times \sqrt{(2)^2+(1)^2+(3)^2} \\ & \quad=\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}} \times \sqrt{14}=1 . \\ & |((2+\lambda) \hat{i}+(-1+3 \lambda) \hat{j}+(-1-\lambda) \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})|=1, \\ & |4+2 \lambda-1+3 \lambda-3-3 \lambda|=1, \\ & |2 \lambda|=1 \Longrightarrow \lambda= \pm \frac{1}{2} \Longrightarrow \lambda^2=\frac{1}{4} . \\ & |v|^2=|a+\lambda b|^2, \\ & |v|^2=|a|^2+\lambda^2|b|^2+2 \lambda(a \cdot b) .\{a \cdot b=0\} \\ & =6+11 \lambda^2+0 . \\ & |v|=\sqrt{6+11 \lambda^2} . \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Put value } \lambda^2=\frac{1}{4} \text { : }\\ &\begin{aligned} & |v|=\sqrt{6+\frac{11}{4}}, \\ & |v|=\sqrt{\frac{24+11}{4}}, \\ & |v|=\sqrt{\frac{35}{4}} \\ & |v|=\frac{\sqrt{35}}{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: Let $\vec{a}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\vec{b}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$. If $\vec{c}$ is a vector such that $2(\vec{a} \times \vec{c})+3(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})=\overrightarrow{0}$ and $(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{c}=-97$, then $|\vec{c} \times \hat{\mathrm{k}}|^2$ is equal to
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Given

$$ \begin{gathered} \vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}, \quad \vec{b}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k} \\ a(\vec{a} \times \vec{c})+3(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})=0 \\ 2 \vec{a} \times \vec{c}+3 \vec{b} \times \vec{c}=0 \\ (2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}) \times \vec{c}=0 \end{gathered} $$

This means $2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}$ are parallel.

$$ \Rightarrow \vec{c}=\lambda(2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}) $$

$$ \vec{c}=\lambda[2(2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})+3(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})] $$

$$ \vec{c}=\lambda(7 \hat{i}-13 \hat{j}+19 \hat{k}) $$

Given

$$ (\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{c}=-97 $$

$$ \begin{gathered} \vec{a}-\vec{b}=(2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})-(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \\ =\hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \end{gathered} $$

Put in equation:

$$ \begin{gathered} (\hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \cdot \lambda(7 \hat{i}-13 \hat{j}+19 \hat{k})=-97 \\ \lambda(7+52+38)=-97 \\ \lambda(97)=-97 \Rightarrow \lambda=-1 \\ \vec{c}=-1(7 \hat{i}-13 \hat{j}+19 \hat{k}) \\ \vec{c}=-7 \hat{i}+13 \hat{j}-19 \hat{k} \\ \vec{c} \times \hat{k}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -7 & 13 & -19 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right| \\ =\hat{i}(13)-\hat{j}(-7)+\hat{k}(0) \\ =13 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j} \\ |\vec{c} \times \hat{k}|^2=13^2+7^2=169+49=218 \end{gathered} $$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.
